clean_story: Seeing inside bodies.  No surgery needed. "Medical imaging" is exactly what it sounds like—tools that create pictures of bones, organs, and tissues while the body stays intact. Like using a flashlight to peek through a foggy window, but with X-rays, magnets, or sound waves instead. X-rays are the oldest trick. A machine shoots invisible light through you—dense stuff like bones blocks it, casting shadows on film. Dentists use it to spot hidden cavities. Airport scanners work the same way, just for luggage. MRI machines skip radiation. They pull hydrogen atoms in your body into alignment with a giant magnet, then nudge them with radio waves. When the atoms snap back, they emit signals—a map of soft tissues, even brain activity. Doctors use MRIs to hunt tumors or torn ligaments without a single cut. Ultrasound bounces high-pitched sound off organs, like sonar for submarines. Gel helps the waves travel—no gel, no picture, just static. Pregnant parents see their baby’s first kicks this way. Three related subjects are body electricity, nanomedicine tools, and sound wave uses.
